seoKeywords: when to see doctor blood pressure
explanation:
**Normal blood pressure ranges** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. These are guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

Normal <120/80. Elevated 120-129/80. Stage 1 Hypertension 130-139/80-89.

This calculator applies the formula using your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside the normal range.

## Normal Reference Ranges

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 (Oxygen Saturation) | 95-100% |
| Fasting Blood Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculator provides guidance only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult with a qualified healthcare professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: What is considered normal blood pressure?
faq.a: Normal blood pressure is less than 120/80 mmHg. Elevated is 120-129 systolic (and less than 80 diastolic). These ranges are based on major population studies like NHANES and WHO guidelines.

faq.q: What do the two blood pressure numbers mean?
faq.a: The first number (systolic) is the pressure when your heart beats; the second (diastolic) is the pressure when your heart rests. Both numbers matter—your blood pressure category is determined by whichever is higher.

Stage 1 Hypertension is systolic 130-139 or diastolic 80-89 mmHg. This typically warrants lifestyle changes and closer monitoring; your doctor may recommend medication depending on your personal health history.

faq.q: When should I see a doctor about high blood pressure?
faq.a: If your readings are consistently above 130/80, if you have symptoms (headaches, shortness of breath, chest pain), or if you have a family history of heart disease or stroke, consult a doctor.

faq.q: Are normal blood pressure ranges the same for everyone?
faq.a: No. Normal ranges vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and individual factors. These guidelines represent population averages—your personal medical history and circumstances always take precedence.

faq.q: Do medications affect blood pressure readings?
faq.a: Yes. Many drugs—including stimulants, decongestants, NSAIDs, and corticosteroids—can raise blood pressure. Always tell your doctor about all medications you're taking when discussing blood pressure.
This calculator provides a screening tool only, based on established clinical guidelines. For diagnosis and treatment decisions, always consult with a qualified healthcare professional.

faq.q: How often should I check my blood pressure?
faq.a: If you're healthy with normal readings, annual checks are usually sufficient. If you have elevated or high readings, family history of hypertension, or other risk factors, check more frequently and consult your doctor.
Our guidelines come from major international health organizations: WHO, NIH (National Institutes of Health), and NHLBI (National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute).
